how much scaffolding is provided beforehand?

node.text: ◆ signal: flash-attn-from-scratch (NumPy FlashAttention) — Explicitly documents that FlashAttention is SLOWER on CPU because NumPy BLAS makes one large matmul beat many small tiled ones — the CPU is compute-bound, so the winning strategy is maximizing BLAS matmul efficiency, NOT tiling.

receipts.quote: explain MoE active-params from scratch

node.text: ◆ signal: Inference-Kernel-Optimization-Lab (NumPy/Numba kernels) — Empirically shows NumPy BLAS hits ~130-140 GFLOPs vs hand-tuned Numba at 3-4 GFLOPs (9x slower) — confirms that on CPU you should route matmuls through np.matmul/BLAS rather than custom JIT kernels for the attention benchmark.

receipts.quote: so it's not reading the whole thing in one go

node.text: ◆ signal: numpy einsum slowness discussion — einsum without optimize=True (or with singleton dims) misses BLAS and is 10-1000x slower; when a contraction has no batch indices, optimize=True routes to tensordot/matmul — so for single-head QK^T and PV, plain np.matmul beats einsum and einsum needs optimize routing to hit BLAS.

receipts.quote: Track four evidence sets ... + offset reads

node.text: ◆ signal: How softmax is implemented in practice — PyTorch's C++ softmax fuses the exp-write and sum in one pass then multiplies by reciprocal of the sum — reducing memory passes and replacing per-element division with a single reciprocal, a concrete numpy micro-opt (multiply by 1/sum instead of divide).

receipts.quote: Reasoning-effort tuning: Medium can beat High ... A cheap knob to sweep

node.text: ◆ signal: Tuning Flash Attention (NVIDIA) — Identifies exp2 and truediv as the special-function bottlenecks and fast-math (approximate exp) as the biggest lever once matmul is handled — suggests a faster approximate exp for the softmax could be the CPU win after BLAS matmul is saturated.

text: scout round 3/3

receipts.quote: fast-math (approximate exp) as the biggest lever once matmul is handled

node.text: ◆ signal: approxmath: fast approximate exp/log for numpy — Drop-in vectorized approximate exp for numpy arrays is 4-5x faster than np.exp at ~3e-9 relative accuracy — directly usable to accelerate the softmax after BLAS matmul is saturated, likely within the benchmark's correctness tolerance.

receipts.quote: first-principles ML pedagogy; the north-star for the 'understand the mechanism' instinct

node.text: ◆ signal: A Tiny Float32 Exponential with Near-Library Accuracy (3-ULP) — A table-free float32 exp using split-ln2 argument reduction + exponent-field bit trick + degree-6 residual polynomial hits ~0.226 mean ULP — a recipe to hand-roll a numpy-vectorizable exp that is both fast and accurate enough to pass a strict correctness gate.

receipts.ref: How softmax is implemented in practice prior harvest
receipts.quote: multiply by reciprocal of the sum — reducing memory passes and replacing per-element division with a single reciprocal

node.text: ◆ signal: Turbo-Softmax: poly5 + IEEE-754 bithack softmax — A full softmax kernel using 2^i via bit manipulation and 5th-order poly for e^t achieves 4x speedup with 100% top-k match and <1e-6 max error — evidence that an approximate softmax preserves argmax/ranking and stays within tight numeric tolerance, exactly the correctness posture the benchmark needs.

receipts.quote: route matmuls through np.matmul/BLAS rather than custom JIT kernels

node.text: ◆ signal: OpenBLAS multithread switch causes slowdown at small sizes — OpenBLAS abruptly switches from 1 to all threads around size ~96 (gemm) causing slowdowns for small matmuls; for a single-head attention with modest seq_len, forcing OPENBLAS_NUM_THREADS=1 (or thread control) may be faster than default multithreading — a free knob to sweep.

receipts.quote: first-principles ML pedagogy; the north-star for the 'understand the mechanism' instinct

node.text: ◆ signal: float32 SIMD faster than float64 in numpy — float32 gets 2x-wider SIMD lanes and (without AVX-512) numpy's float64 transcendentals lack SIMD, so computing attention in float32 speeds both matmul and exp — but the win depends on whether numpy was built with SVML/AVX-512, worth verifying on the benchmark machine.

receipts.quote: where numpy CPU time actually goes (matmul FLOPs vs memory traffic)

node.text: ◆ signal: NumPy MKL vs OpenBLAS benchmark — MKL is faster than OpenBLAS across most linear algebra ops and both default to using all cores; thread count and BLAS backend choice materially change matmul time, so pinning BLAS/threads is a first-order lever for the attention benchmark.

receipts.ref: How softmax is implemented in practice prior harvest
receipts.quote: fuses the exp-write and sum in one pass then multiplies by reciprocal of the sum

node.text: ◆ signal: scipy softmax/log_softmax reference implementation — scipy's softmax does max-shift then exp then sum — the exact stable reference; but it allocates several temporaries, so a fused in-place numpy version (shift, exp in-place, multiply by 1/sum) can beat calling scipy.special.softmax directly.

node.text: Avoid boolean-mask fancy indexing; apply causal mask via additive triangular float array (np.triu on a -inf matrix added to scores) (micro-optimization)

text: bench: Avoid boolean-mask fancy indexing; apply causal mask via additive triangular float array (np.triu on a -inf matrix added to scores)

node.text: Avoid per-iteration online-softmax rescaling branch by hoisting the acc rescale: since scores for causal attention over K rows are unordered, replace the branchy running-max update with a simpler always-scale formulation using max only, keeping the QK dot product but removing the acc-array rescale in the common (s<=m) branch which is already there — instead reduce exp() calls by computing running max cheaply. Concretely, keep online softmax but store acc in a local register-friendly manner and use fastmath exp; main change: precompute nothing extra but mark inner k-loop to help SIMD by using float32 accumulation only and reordering to allow vectorization. (micro-optimization)

text: bench: Avoid per-iteration online-softmax rescaling branch by hoisting the acc rescale: since scores for causal attention over K rows are unordered, replace the branchy running-max update with a simpler always-scale formulation using max only, keeping the QK dot product but removing the acc-array rescale in the common (s<=m) branch which is already there — instead reduce exp() calls by computing running max cheaply. Concretely, keep online softmax but store acc in a local register-friendly manner and use fastmath exp; main change: precompute nothing extra but mark inner k-loop to help SIMD by using float32 accumulation only and reordering to allow vectorization.
